segments.text:
Section 3.6
CYCLOPENTANE

Conformers of Cyclopentane
The planar conformation of cyclopentane has little angle strain, but considerable torsional strain due to eclipsing C–H bonds
The envelope and half-chair conformers involve less torsional strain and are of similar energy
Equilibration between these conformers is rapid

Section 3.7
CONFORMATIONS OF CYCLOHEXANE

Conformers of Cyclohexane
To achieve bond angles of 109.5 degree and minimize torsional strain, cyclohexane adopts a chair conformation
In the chair conformation, all adjacent C–H bonds are staggered

Drawing Chair Conformers
To draw a chair, start with two parallel lines slanted to the right or left

Drawing Chair Conformers-1
To draw a chair, start with two parallel lines slanted to the right or left
Add a second set of parallel lines at an angle of about 120 degree to the first

Drawing Chair Conformers-2
To draw a chair, start with two parallel lines slanted to the right or left
Add a second set of parallel lines at an angle of about 120 degree to the first
Connect the two three-carbon fragments using a third set of parallel lines
The chair conformer is the most stable for cyclohexane, but there are others.

Drawing Chair Conformers-3
To draw a chair, start with two parallel lines slanted to the right or left
Add a second set of parallel lines at an angle of about 120 degree to the first
Connect the two three-carbon fragments using a third set of parallel lines
We can also draw a chair “leaning” the opposite way.

Boat and Skew-boat
The boat conformer is a high-energy structure containing eclipsing C–H bonds
The skew-boat relieves some of the torsional strain in the boat
Both are considerably higher in energy than the chair conformer

Section 3.8
AXIAL AND EQUATORIAL BONDS IN CYCLOHEXANE

Hydrogens in Cyclohexane Chairs
The hydrogens (or substituents) in a cyclohexane chair can be divided into two groups
Half are pointing straight up or down ( axial )
Half are aligned with the carbons ( equatorial )
Axial hydrogens are anti to one another; equatorial hydrogens are gauche to one another

Drawing Axial and Equatorial Groups
Begin by drawing an axial group straight up or down
The axial bond must point in the direction of the “point” formed by the two C–C bonds!
Axial bonds alternate up and down

Drawing Axial and Equatorial Groups - 1
Place the equatorial bonds so as to approximate a tetrahedral arrangement of the bonds to each carbon.
The equatorial bond of each carbon should be parallel to the ring bonds of its two nearest neighbor carbons.
Place equatorial bond at C-1 so that it is parallel to the bonds between C-2 and C-3 and between C-5 and C-6.

Drawing Axial and Equatorial Groups-2
Equatorial bonds should be parallel to the ring bonds on the two nearest neighbor carbons
Note how the colored bonds are parallel to one another.
Drawing the equatorial groups this way ensures that the tetrahedral geometry is represented faithfully

Section 3.9
CONFORMATIONAL INVERSION IN CYCLOHEXANE

Chair Inversion in Cyclohexane
“Right-leaning” and “left-leaning” chair conformers can interconvert rapidly via ring inversion or chair flip
An “up” carbon becomes a “down” carbon and vice versa upon inversion
Axial groups become equatorial and vice versa
However, “up” substituents are still up and “down” substituents are still down!
Potential Energies of Chair Inversion

Axial-Equatorial Switch
The most important point about chair inversion is that axial groups become equatorial and vice versa
Do note however that X and Y are still both pointing up!

Section 3.10
CONFORMATIONAL ANALYSIS OF MONOSUBSTITUTED CYCLOHEXANES

Methylcyclohexane: Distinct Chairs
The two chairs of methylcyclohexane are not equivalent!
In one the methyl group is axial …
…and in the other equatorial
At equilibrium, the concentration of the CH 3 - eq conformer is much higher than that of the CH 3 - ax conformer .

Methylcyclohexane: Distinct Chairs-1
The axial conformer contains significant van der Waals strain between the axial CH 3 and hydrogens at the 3 and 5 positions
The equatorial conformer lacks destabilizing 1,3-diaxial interactions .

Other Substituted Cyclohexanes
Other monosubstituted cyclohexanes follow a similar pattern: the equatorial conformer is more stable than the axial conformer
∆ G degree and K for interconversion depend on the size of the group (larger group = greater ∆ G degree = greater K )
∆G°298 K | ∆G°298 K
Substituent X | Percent axial | Percent equatorial | K | kJ/mol | Kcal/mol
—CH3 | 5 | 95 | 19 | −7.2 | −1.74
—CH2CH3 | 4.6 | 95.4 | 21 | −7.5 | −1.79
—CH(CH3)2 | 3 | 97 | 38 | −9.0 | −2.15
—C(CH3)3 | <0.01 | >99.99 | >9999 | −22.8 | −5.5
—F | 40 | 60 | 1.5 | −1.0 | −0.25
—Cl | 29 | 71 | 2.4 | −2.2 | −0.52
—OH | 17 | 83 | 4.8 | −3.9 | −0.93
—NH2 | 9 | 91 | 11 | −5.9 | −1.4
